name: Patroclus
description: Patroclus, in Greek mythology, is most prominently known as the close companion and, according to some interpretations, the lover of Achilles. While not a deity himself, his significance in the *Iliad* and the broader Trojan War narrative elevates him to a figure of immense importance, influencing the actions of gods and mortals alike. He was the son of Menoetius, a Thessalian king, and either Polymele or Sthenele. Due to an accidental killing in his youth, Patroclus was sent to the court of Peleus, Achilles' father, where he formed an unbreakable bond with the young hero. He is often depicted as a compassionate and loyal figure, offering counsel and support to Achilles throughout the Trojan War. His death at the hands of Hector, while wearing Achilles' armor, is a pivotal moment in the *Iliad*, triggering Achilles' rage and his return to battle, ultimately leading to Hector's demise and the fall of Troy. Patroclus's funeral games are described at length, highlighting the respect and grief felt for him by the Achaean army. His role underscores the themes of friendship, loyalty, and the tragic consequences of war.
subtitle: Companion of Achilles

keyMyths.title: The Death of Patroclus
keyMyths.description: Patroclus's death is perhaps his most significant myth. He donned Achilles' armor to rally the demoralized Achaean forces and drive the Trojans away from the ships. Hector, mistaking Patroclus for Achilles, killed him in battle. This act ignited Achilles' fury and propelled him back into the war, seeking vengeance for his fallen comrade. The grief and rage of Achilles over Patroclus's death is central to the *Iliad*'s plot and themes.

keyMyths.title: Patroclus's Youth and Exile
keyMyths.description: As a young man, Patroclus accidentally killed Clysonymus during a game of dice, leading to his exile from his homeland. He sought refuge with Peleus, Achilles' father, where he grew up alongside Achilles. This event highlights the importance of hospitality and refuge in ancient Greek society, and it sets the stage for the deep bond that formed between Patroclus and Achilles. It also showcases the concept of accidental manslaughter and its consequences.

keyMyths.title: Funeral Games of Patroclus
keyMyths.description: After Hector's death, Achilles organized elaborate funeral games in honor of Patroclus. These games, described in detail in the *Iliad*, included chariot races, boxing matches, wrestling, and archery contests. The games served as a way to honor Patroclus's memory and to provide catharsis for the Achaean warriors. They also offer valuable insights into the athletic competitions and funerary rituals of ancient Greece.

Features a tragic battle between close companions, similar to Patroclus and Achilles.
Illustrates a deep bond between two male figures, one serving as a guide and protector to the other.

worshipPractices.name: Funeral Rites
worshipPractices.description: In ancient Greece, heroes were often honored with elaborate funeral rites, including sacrifices and games. Patroclus's funeral pyre and the subsequent games held in his honor exemplify this practice, signifying the importance of commemorating heroic deeds and ensuring the deceased's passage to the afterlife.

cultural.modernLegacy: Patroclus's legacy endures in literature, art, and popular culture, often as a symbol of deep friendship and, in some interpretations, homosexual love. He remains a powerful figure in discussions of male relationships and the complexities of human emotion.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: Patroclus was the son of Menoetius, king of Opus, and either Sthenele, Philomela, or Polymele. As a young boy, Patroclus was exiled from Opus after accidentally killing Clysonymus in a dispute over dice. Menoetius took Patroclus to the court of King Peleus in Phthia, where he was raised alongside Achilles. This shared upbringing fostered a deep and enduring bond between the two young men. Patroclus became Achilles's squire and close confidant, sharing his training, his tent, and his innermost thoughts. This early period of their lives solidified a relationship that would profoundly impact the course of the Trojan War. Their connection was one of mutual respect, loyalty, and affection, setting the stage for the tragic events that would later unfold on the plains of Troy. The circumstances of Patroclus's exile shaped his character, instilling in him a sense of humility and a deep appreciation for the second chance he was given.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: While not a central figure in many independent quests, Patroclus's significance lies in his role within the Trojan War. His defining moment came when Achilles, enraged by a dispute with Agamemnon, withdrew from battle. As the Trojan forces, led by Hector, pushed the Achaean army to the brink of defeat, Patroclus pleaded with Achilles to rejoin the fight. When Achilles refused, Patroclus, with Achilles' permission, donned Achilles's armor and led the Myrmidons into battle. This act of courage and self-sacrifice turned the tide of the war, driving the Trojans back to the gates of Troy. However, Patroclus's triumph was short-lived. He was eventually killed by Hector, who stripped him of Achilles's armor. Patroclus's death ignited Achilles's fury and set him on a path of vengeance, leading to Hector's demise and ultimately contributing to the fall of Troy. His willingness to risk his life for his comrades cemented his status as a hero and a symbol of selfless devotion.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: Patroclus's legacy is deeply intertwined with that of Achilles. His death serves as the catalyst for Achilles's return to battle and his subsequent actions, shaping the narrative of the *Iliad*. Patroclus is remembered as a symbol of loyalty, friendship, and self-sacrifice. His relationship with Achilles has been interpreted in various ways throughout history, ranging from a close platonic bond to a romantic love affair. Regardless of interpretation, their connection remains one of the most compelling and enduring aspects of Greek mythology. Patroclus's story continues to resonate in modern literature, art, and film, serving as an exploration of human relationships, the cost of war, and the complexities of grief and revenge. He remains a poignant reminder of the power of human connection and the enduring impact of loss.

shortDescription: Companion of Achilles, Patroclus' death in the Trojan War spurred Achilles to avenge him and turn the tide of battle. A symbol of loyalty and sacrifice.
